Elias Range Stays

The Housing-Retention Connection

How where you live determines whether you stay — and what it costs when you leave.

The Silent Crisis

Housing isn't just a recruitment problem. It's a retention crisis hiding in plain sight.

The data points to housing as the #1 barrier to rural healthcare recruitment. But the real story isn't in the numbers — it's in the pattern. Nurses who feel isolated leave. Physicians who feel unsafe don't come back. Workers who have nowhere to decompress burn out faster.

The connection between housing quality and clinical retention isn't a theory. It's a documented, repeatable cycle that health authorities are only beginning to acknowledge.

Northern Health explicitly identified housing as "one of the key challenges towards recruitment" — 2024
NB health networks spent $120M+ on travel nurses in 2022–24 — direct result of retention failure
Fort St. John ER: 5 closures in a single week. Chetwynd: 25 service interruptions in 2024
GoHealth BC provides ALL accommodation to travel nurses — proof housing is inseparable from the value proposition
The Framework

The Client Road Map — from Sharp Pain to Stability

Tier 1
Sharp Pain — The Acute Crisis
The Problem
  • Isolation — single workers in unfamiliar communities with no social infrastructure
  • Safety concerns — poor quality, asbestos, "wall to wall" single rooms
  • Broken expectations from day one
What It Looks Like
  • Worker arrives. Reality does not match what was promised.
  • Disengagement sets in within days, not weeks.
  • Early departure — contract abandoned or not renewed.
Result: Broken expectations, rapid disengagement, early departure.
Tier 2
Pain — The Grinding Reality
  • No community connection — nothing to return to after a shift
  • No transport, no access to amenities, hours from anything familiar
  • Financial insecurity compounding housing insecurity
Result: Workers don't recommend the posting. Word spreads. Recruitment dries up.
Tier 3
Bearings — What Stability Looks Like
  • Modern, furnished, clean accommodation in good locations
  • Walking distance to work, services, outdoor spaces
  • A sense of place — not just a room
Result: Workers complete contracts, return for future postings, and recommend the opportunity.
The Cycle

Poor housing doesn't just cost one nurse. It costs every nurse who hears about it.

80%
of healthcare workers feel isolated on posting
60%
are burned out — housing quality is a direct contributor
$30K+
signing bonuses can't overcome housing as a barrier
1→∞
one nurse who leaves takes their review with them — word of mouth cuts both ways

The retention cycle is not complicated. A worker arrives. The housing is poor. They feel isolated. They don't feel supported. They leave early — or they finish the contract and never come back. They tell colleagues. Recruitment for that posting becomes harder. The health authority spends more on agency costs. The cycle repeats.

Breaking the cycle requires one thing: housing that makes people feel genuinely at home. Not a hotel room. Not a bunkhouse. A community.

The Solution Mirrors the Problem

Every problem has an exact reversal.

Community

Long table dinners, weekly events, spaces designed for belonging. The Draw Close program. Not programmed wellness — genuine community infrastructure.

Connections

Walking distance to work, to downtown, to parks and trails. Not isolated. Embedded in the place.

Experiences

Modern, fresh environments. Outdoor access. A life worth coming back to — not just a posting to endure.

Northern Health invested significantly to ensure nurses 'look forward to coming back.' That's not a nice-to-have. That's the retention strategy.

— Northern Health, 2024
The Lived Evidence

158 locum positions. The data lives in the experience.

Dr. Rob Johnson has completed 158 locum positions across British Columbia. He has slept in the rooms. He has felt the isolation. He has experienced the difference between a posting you endure and one you want to come back to.

This framework isn't theoretical for Rob — it's autobiographical. As ERS's Healthcare Advisor, Rob's role is to pressure-test this model, validate the assumptions, and ensure that every housing decision ERS makes is grounded in the real experience of the professionals we serve.

"Does this map to what you've lived? Where does it need to change? What data from your experience can sharpen this?" — that's the conversation.

"To serve those who provide health to others."
